one_line_takeaway: BADSKILL shows that a third-party agent skill bundling a backdoor-fine-tuned classifier can hide a compositional, parameter-conditioned trigger that fires with up to 99.5% ASR at near-zero benign-accuracy cost, reframing skill installation as a model supply-chain problem that code inspection and benign-accuracy checks cannot catch.
key_finding: Model-in-skill backdoors are highly effective and stealthy across model families and scales. Clean skills achieve 100% BA and 0% ASR, confirming the attack is introduced by the poison-and-package process. Once backdoored, all eight models reach average ASR of 97.5%-99.5% (averaged over all 8 triggered skills) while the largest BA drop is only 4.2 points and more than half of model-skill pairs retain perfect benign accuracy. The attack is poison-efficient: a 3% poison rate already yields 91.7% ASR, with ASR rising sharply between 1% and 3% and saturating around 7% while BA stays above 87% throughout the 1%-20% sweep. Trigger complexity has a sweet spot: 2-3 field triggers consistently achieve ASR >= 95% across Qwen2.5-0.5B/1.5B/3B, whereas single-field triggers are too weak and >=4-field triggers over-specialize. The trigger degrades gracefully (not catastrophically) under five surface perturbations, with the worst case being 10% typo injection (average ASR ~80.7%). A central security implication: benign-side accuracy is a weak standalone screening signal because a skill can behave normally while still encoding a highly effective trigger-conditioned policy.
core_contribution: The paper introduces BADSKILL, a backdoor-attack formulation for a new threat surface the authors call 'model-in-skill poisoning': agent 'skills' (installable modular packages) that bundle a learned model artifact as part of their execution logic. The paper's own contributions are three-fold: (1) it formalizes the model-in-skill backdoor threat, in which an adversary embeds a backdoor-fine-tuned binary classifier inside an ostensibly benign third-party skill so that malicious behavior is carried by the installed artifact itself rather than by prompts, retrieved context, or tool outputs; (2) it proposes a two-stage attack design tailored to skills that combines compositional semantic triggers over structured skill parameters, hard negatives, and a composite training objective; and (3) it provides a case-study empirical evaluation in an OpenClaw-inspired simulation environment across 8 model architectures and 8 triggered skill categories. Note: prior backdoor/prompt-injection/agent-poisoning work (e.g., AgentPoison, weight poisoning, instruction-tuning backdoors) is cited as Related Work to contrast the new carrier (the skill artifact) and is NOT part of this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Gray-box, supply-chain adversary acting only at skill-development time. The attacker knows the public skill interface, packaging format, installation workflow, and how the skill category is typically used, and may start from a public pre-trained LLM; the attacker does NOT know the victim's private prompts, conversation history, or host-side runtime configuration and cannot modify the core agent runtime, gateway LLM, or host privilege model. Capabilities: design the skill interface/parser schema, construct poisoned and hard-negative training queries, fine-tune a compact embedded classifier, and distribute the model inside an otherwise ordinary skill bundle through the normal installation channel. Two attacker objectives: (1) Effectiveness — trigger-aligned queries route execution to a hidden payload branch; (2) Benign-side preservation — ordinary queries retain advertised functionality so routine use and lightweight manual inspection do not reveal the compromise. Activation depends on a hidden conjunction over multiple individually benign structured parameters. In this study the payload is deliberately restricted to a benign canary action (writing a timestamped marker file).
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: (1) models evaluated only up to 7.1B parameters, so larger-scale behavior is unknown; (2) experiments run in a lightweight simulated skill environment rather than across production agent stacks; (3) no dedicated defense evaluation, so detection/mitigation effectiveness remains open; (4) study restricted to English triggers and a benign canary payload (timestamped marker file), so real malicious payloads and broader threat space are not exercised. Reviewer-added: there is no external attack baseline because the setting is new (only clean-skill vs BadSkill and loss-component ablations are compared), and the embedded 'model' is a compact binary router classifier, not a full generative model, which bounds the demonstrated payload richness.

datasets_used: Custom BADSKILL benchmark: 13 skills total (8 triggered, 5 non-trigger control skills)

Combined main evaluation set: 571 negative-class queries + 396 trigger-aligned queries (of the 571 negatives, 385 from the 8 triggered skills including 16 held-out hard negatives, 186 from the 5 control skills)
Full training corpus: 5,073 query instances across the 13 skills, with six paraphrastic augmentations; default poison rate 7%; 52 hard-negative base templates
8 triggered skill tasks: Note Cleanup, Summarizer, Unit Converter, CSV Processor, Email Drafter, JSON Transformer, Text Formatter, Calendar Formatter (trigger arity 2-3)
5 non-trigger control skills: translator (40), regex_helper (29), markdown_converter (33), list_sorter (29), general (55)
quantitative_results: Clean baselines: 100% BA and 0% ASR on all skills
Backdoored average ASR ranges 97.5%-99.5% across the 8 models (InternLM2.5-1.8B highest at 99.5%; Phi-3.5-mini lowest at 97.5%; primary Qwen2.5-0.5B 99.2%)
Largest benign-accuracy drop after backdooring: 4.2 points (Qwen2.5-0.5B, 95.8% BA)
Poison efficiency: 3% poison rate yields 91.7% ASR; ASR saturates ~7%; BA stays above 87% across 1%-20% sweep
Trigger complexity: 2-3 field (conjunction) triggers achieve ASR >= 95% across Qwen2.5-0.5B/1.5B/3B
Robustness (Qwen2.5-0.5B): average ASR 88.6% under Typo 5%, 80.7% under Typo 10%, 95.4% under Word Swap 10%, 92.3% under Char Del 5%, 94.2% under Char Ins 5% (Clean 98.9%)
Loss ablation (Qwen2.5-0.5B): Full objective 99.0% avg ASR; removing L_poison drops to 98.0% (-1.0); L_cls only 98.5% (-0.5) and less stable on structured skills like Unit Converter
Model scale: 8 architectures spanning 494M-7.1B parameters from 5 families; primary model file 1.9 GB safetensors, trained in ~556 s (~9.3 min), best val F1 = 0.9979
baselines_compared: Clean skill (unpoisoned) vs BadSkill (backdoored) for every model-skill pair — the primary comparison
Poison-rate sweep 1%-20%; five text-perturbation types (Typo 5/10%, Word Swap 10%, Char Deletion 5%, Char Insertion 5%)
No external prior-attack baseline (the model-in-skill setting is newly formalized; prior backdoor/agent-poisoning methods appear only as Related Work for contrast)
